request.question: With the Patriots, D. J. Foster won an American football game that was played on Sunday, February 5, 2017, in what stadium?

title: Brian Flores
D. J. Foster
Super Bowl LI
Super Bowl XXXIX
Jerry Schuplinski
Nick Caley
Super Bowl XLII
Super Bowl XXXVIII
Josh Boyer
Super Bowl XLVI
sentences: Brian Flores (born February 24, 1981) is a current American football linebackers coach for the New England Patriots of the National Football League (NFL).
After attending Poly Prep Country Day School in Brooklyn, New York, Flores played football at Boston College as a linebacker from 1999 through 2003.
In 2004, Flores joined the Patriots as a scouting assistant.
He became a pro scout in 2006 before moving to the coaching staff as a special teams coaching assistant in 2008.
His title was changed to assistant coach offense/special teams in 2010.
He was named defensive assistant in 2011.
In 2012, he was named safeties coach.
He was named linebackers coach ahead of the 2016 season.
On February 5, 2017, Flores was part of the Patriots coaching staff that won Super Bowl LI.
In the game, the Patriots defeated the Atlanta Falcons by a score of 34-28 in overtime.
D. J. Foster (born November 22, 1993) is an American football running back for the Arizona Cardinals of the National Football League (NFL).
He played college football at Arizona State University.
He signed with the New England Patriots as an undrafted free agent in 2016.
With the Patriots, he won Super Bowl LI against the Atlanta Falcons.
Super Bowl LI was an American football game played at NRG Stadium in Houston, Texas, on Sunday, February 5, 2017, to determine the champion of the National Football League (NFL) for the 2016 season.

The American Football Conference (AFC) champion New England Patriots, after trailing by as many as 25 points (28-3) during the third quarter, defeated the National Football Conference (NFC) champion Atlanta Falcons, 34–28 in overtime.
The Patriots' 25-point comeback is the largest comeback in Super Bowl history, and Super Bowl LI was the first to be decided in overtime.

Super Bowl XXXIX was an American football game played between the American Football Conference (AFC) champion New England Patriots and the National Football Conference (NFC) champion Philadelphia Eagles to decide the National Football League (NFL) champion for the 2004 season.
The Patriots defeated the Eagles by the score of 24–21.
The game was played on February 6, 2005, at Alltel Stadium (now known as EverBank Field) in Jacksonville, Florida, the first time the Super Bowl was played in that city.
Jerry Schuplinski is an American football coach who is the current assistant quarterbacks coach for the New England Patriots of the National Football League (NFL).
Jerry was an assistant coach for Case Western Reserve for six seasons before becoming a part of the Patriots' staff in 2013.
On February 5, 2017, Schuplinski was part of the Patriots coaching staff that won Super Bowl LI.
In the game, the Patriots defeated the Atlanta Falcons by a score of 34–28 in overtime.
Nick Caley is an American football coach who is the current tight ends coach of the New England Patriots of the National Football League (NFL).
He coached at the collegiate level for 11 seasons before becoming part of the Patriots' staff in 2013.
On February 5, 2017, Caley was part of the Patriots coaching staff that won Super Bowl LI.
In the game, the Patriots defeated the Atlanta Falcons by a score of 34–28 in overtime.

Super Bowl XLII was an American football game between the National Football Conference (NFC) champion New York Giants and the American Football Conference (AFC) champion New England Patriots to decide the National Football League (NFL) champion for the 2007 season.
The Giants defeated the Patriots by the score of 17–14.
The game was played on February 3, 2008, at University of Phoenix Stadium in Glendale, Arizona.

Super Bowl XXXVIII was an American football game between the National Football Conference (NFC) champion Carolina Panthers and the American Football Conference (AFC) champion New England Patriots to decide the National Football League (NFL) champion for the 2003 season.
The Patriots defeated the Panthers by the score of 32-29.
The game was played at Reliant Stadium in Houston, Texas, on February 1, 2004.
At the time, this was the most watched Super Bowl ever with 144.4 million viewers.
Josh Boyer (born January 21, 1977) is an American football coach in the National Football League, currently serving as cornerbacks coach for the New England Patriots.

After graduating from Muskingum College, where he played football as a wide receiver and defensive back, he began his coaching career in 2000 as a graduate assistant at King's College, Pennsylvania, and then served in the same capacity at the University of Dayton in 2001 and Kent State University in 2002 and 2003.
In 2004, Boyer served as the defensive backs coach at Bryant University.
He then served as the defensive coordinator at the South Dakota School of Mines and Technology in 2005 before joining the New England Patriots in 2006 as a defensive coaching assistant.
He was promoted to defensive backs coach following the 2008 season.
In 2012, his title was changed to cornerbacks coach.
On February 5, 2017, Boyer was part of the Patriots coaching staff that won Super Bowl LI.
In the game, the Patriots defeated the Atlanta Falcons by a score of 34–28 in overtime.

Super Bowl XLVI was an American football game between the National Football Conference (NFC) champion New York Giants and the American Football Conference (AFC) champion New England Patriots to decide the National Football League (NFL) champion for the 2011 season.
The Giants defeated the Patriots by the score of 21–17.
The game was played on February 5, 2012, at Lucas Oil Stadium in Indianapolis, the first time that the Super Bowl was played in the state of Indiana.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

The American Football Conference (AFC) champion New England Patriots, after trailing by as many as 25 points (28-3) during the third quarter, defeated the National Football Conference (NFC) champion Atlanta Falcons, 34–28 in overtime.  The Patriots' 25-point comeback is the largest comeback in Super Bowl history, and Super Bowl LI was the first to be decided in overtime.

The Patriots defeated the Eagles by the score of 24–21.  The game was played on February 6, 2005, at Alltel Stadium (now known as EverBank Field) in Jacksonville, Florida, the first time the Super Bowl was played in that city.

Super Bowl XLVI | Super Bowl XLVI was an American football game between the National Football Conference (NFC) champion New York Giants and the American Football Conference (AFC) champion New England Patriots to decide the National Football League (NFL) champion for the 2011 season.  The Giants defeated the Patriots by the score of 21–17.  The game was played on February 5, 2012, at Lucas Oil Stadium in Indianapolis, the first time that the Super Bowl was played in the state of Indiana.

Super Bowl XLII | Super Bowl XLII was an American football game between the National Football Conference (NFC) champion New York Giants and the American Football Conference (AFC) champion New England Patriots to decide the National Football League (NFL) champion for the 2007 season.  The Giants defeated the Patriots by the score of 17–14.  The game was played on February 3, 2008, at University of Phoenix Stadium in Glendale, Arizona.

Nick Caley | Nick Caley is an American football coach who is the current tight ends coach of the New England Patriots of the National Football League (NFL).  He coached at the collegiate level for 11 seasons before becoming part of the Patriots' staff in 2013.  On February 5, 2017, Caley was part of the Patriots coaching staff that won Super Bowl LI.  In the game, the Patriots defeated the Atlanta Falcons by a score of 34–28 in overtime.

He played college football at Arizona State University.  He signed with the New England Patriots as an undrafted free agent in 2016.  With the Patriots, he won Super Bowl LI against the Atlanta Falcons.

- "Sunday, February 5, 2017" — [1]: "Sunday, February 5, 2017"
- "New England Patriots" — [1]: "New England Patriots"
- "Atlanta Falcons" — [1]: "Atlanta Falcons"

Ambiguities:
- None identified; "Super Bowl LI", date, and "NRG Stadium" are consistently stated.

Bridge / missing fact:
- All required facts present and unambiguous: stadium for the game D. J. Foster won (Super Bowl LI) is "NRG Stadium".
- Final conclusion: NRG Stadium.

Retrieval direction:
- "Super Bowl LI" (page) — verify stadium and game details.
- "D. J. Foster" (page) — verify participation/winning credit.
- "NRG Stadium" (page) — venue details.
- "New England Patriots" (Super Bowl LI roster/participation) — if roster confirmation is needed.
hop2_docs: Super Bowl LI halftime show | The Super Bowl LI Halftime show took place on February 5, 2017, at NRG Stadium in Houston, Texas as part of Super Bowl LI.  The show was headlined by Lady Gaga, who performed a medley of her songs, including newer material from her most recent studio album "Joanne".

Super Bowl LI | Super Bowl LI was an American football game played at NRG Stadium in Houston, Texas, on Sunday, February 5, 2017, to determine the champion of the National Football League (NFL) for the 2016 season.  The American Football Conference (AFC) champion New England Patriots, after trailing by as many as 25 points (28-3) during the third quarter, defeated the National Football Conference (NFC) champion Atlanta Falcons, 34–28 in overtime.  The Patriots' 25-point comeback is the largest comeback in Super Bowl history, and Super Bowl LI was the first to be decided in overtime.

2017 NFL season | The 2017 NFL season, the 98th and current season in the history of the National Football League (NFL), began on September 7, 2017, with the Kansas City Chiefs defeating the defending Super Bowl LI champion New England Patriots 42–27 in the NFL Kickoff Game.  The season will conclude with Super Bowl LII, the league's championship game, on February 4, 2018, at U.S. Bank Stadium in Minneapolis, Minnesota.

2016 NFL season | The 2016 NFL season, the 97th season in the history of the National Football League (NFL), began on September 8, 2016, with the defending Super Bowl 50 champion Denver Broncos defeating the Carolina Panthers 21–20 in the NFL Kickoff Game.  The season concluded with Super Bowl LI, the league's championship game on February 5, 2017, at NRG Stadium in Houston, Texas with the New England Patriots defeating the Atlanta Falcons 34–28 in overtime.

Super Bowl Sunday | Super Bowl Sunday is the day on which the Super Bowl, the annual championship for the National Football League, is played.  It is usually on the last Sunday in January or the first Sunday in February and is sometimes referred to as an unofficial national holiday.  The 51st annual event, Super Bowl LI, occurred on February 5, 2017.  Festivities for Super Bowl Sunday typically involve groups of people gathering to watch the game.  Both "Super Bowl Sunday" and "Super Sunday" are registered trademarks of the National Football League.

2016–17 NFL playoffs | The National Football League playoffs for the 2016 NFL season began on Saturday, January 7, 2017.  The postseason tournament concluded on Sunday, February 5, 2017, with Super Bowl LI at NRG Stadium in Houston, Texas.

Super Bowl XXXII | Super Bowl XXXII was an American football game between the National Football Conference (NFC) champion Green Bay Packers and the American Football Conference (AFC) champion Denver Broncos to decide the National Football League (NFL) champion for the 1997 season.  The Broncos defeated the Packers by the score of 31–24.  The game was played on January 25, 1998 at Qualcomm Stadium in San Diego, California, the second time that the Super Bowl was held in that city.  Super Bowl XXXII also made Qualcomm Stadium the only stadium in history to have the Super Bowl and the World Series in the same year.
response.summary_2: D. J. Foster won Super Bowl LI, which was played at NRG Stadium in Houston, Texas.
